coredata.dc:title: Adsorption and sensing of SF6 decomposition gases by ruthenium-decorated silicon-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Saurav
Agnihotri, Neha
coredata.dc:description: Gas-insulated switchgear (GIS) equipment must be safeguarded by detecting and eliminating the toxic decomposition components of SF6 partial discharge. The adsorption and sensing mechanisms of five typical SF6 decomposition gases, i.e. H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2, along with the background gas SF6, on the surface of ruthenium-decorated silicon-doped defective graphene quantum dots (Ru@3Si-GQD) have been investigated using density functional theory and the quantum theory of atoms in molecules. The adsorption energy, charge transfer, band gap, density of states, work function, sensitivity, and desorption time have been evaluated. The Ru@3Si-GQD adsorbent exhibited chemisorption, demonstrating high adsorption energies as well as excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards SO2 and SOF2 gases at 298 K. Additionally, Ru@3Si-GQD shows significant potential for use in sensing applications and is a promising biocompatible material for the development of efficient gas sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SF6 decomposition gases
Silicon doped GQD
DFT
Adsorption
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural analysis of Ru@3Si-GQD and target gases
Quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and electron localization function (ELF) analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), New Delhi, India , for the research grant.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures of the surface (a) 555–777 GQD, (b) 3Si-GQD, (c) Ru@3Si-GQD, and the target gases i.e. (d) SF6, (e) H2S, (f) HF, (g) SO2, (h) SO2F2 and (h) SOF2.
Optimized structures and adsorption distances after the adsorption of the target gases i.e. (a) SF6, (b) H2S, (c) HF, (d) SO2, (e) SO2F2 and (f) SOF2 on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD respectively.
Variation of adsorption energy and adsorption distance for the adsorption of the target gases i.e. SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2 and SOF2 on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD.

The plots of the reduced density gradient (s) versus the sign of the second eigen value of the electron-density Hessian matrix (λ 2) times electron density sign (λ 2) ρ for the adsorption of the target gases i.e. (a) SF6, (b) H2S, (c) HF, (d) SO2, (e) SO2F2 and (f) SOF2 on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD respectively.

Charge Density Difference (CDD) plots after the adsorption of the target gases i.e. (a) SF6, (b) H2S, (c) HF, (d) SO2, (e) SO2F2 and (f) SOF2 on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD respectively. The yellow and cyan colour represents the charge density accumulation and depletion regions respectively.

Variation in HOMO and LUMO orbitals/ energy levels of Ru@3Si-GQD surface before and after the adsorption of the target gases i.e. SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2 and SOF2. The red and green colour represents the positive and negative electron density respectively.
Density of states (DOS) curves before and after the adsorption of the target gases i.e. (a) SF6, (b) H2S, (c) HF, (d) SO2, (e) SO2F2 and (f) SOF2 on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD respectively.

Description of topological parameters at bond critical points (BCP) after the adsorption of target gases i.e. SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2 and SOF2 on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD obtained by QTAIM analysis.

Variation in energy band gap (|∆E
g
|), work function (|ΔФ|) sensitivity (S) and desorption time (τ) after the adsorption of target gases i.e. SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2 and SOF2 on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD.

head.ce:title: Adsorption and sensing of SF6 decomposition gases by ruthenium-decorated silicon-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Jamshedpur 831014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Jamshedpur-831014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gas-insulated switchgear (GIS) equipment must be safeguarded by detecting and eliminating the toxic decomposition components of SF6 partial discharge. The adsorption and sensing mechanisms of five typical SF6 decomposition gases, i.e. H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2, along with the background gas SF6, on the surface of ruthenium-decorated silicon-doped defective graphene quantum dots (Ru@3Si-GQD) have been investigated using density functional theory and the quantum theory of atoms in molecules. The adsorption energy, charge transfer, band gap, density of states, work function, sensitivity, and desorption time have been evaluated. The Ru@3Si-GQD adsorbent exhibited chemisorption, demonstrating high adsorption energies as well as excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards SO2 and SOF2 gases at 298 K. Additionally, Ru@3Si-GQD shows significant potential for use in sensing applications and is a promising biocompatible material for the development of efficient gas sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The gas-insulated switchgear (GIS) used in electrical power systems mainly employs SF6 gas as its insulating medium due to its exceptional stability, outstanding insulating capabilities, and effective arc-extinguishing characteristics [1]. However, over extended periods of operation, the insulation strength of GIS reduces due to partial discharge phenomena. This decline leads to the breakdown of SF6 into low-fluorine sulfides, which can then react with oxygen and moisture to produce byproducts such as H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2 and SOF2 [1,2]. The byproducts act as indicators of the equipment's operational status and can potentially damage GIS infrastructure and pose health risks to personnel [3]. While the detection of partial discharge is challenging, the use of SF6 decomposed gas detection is a primary method for monitoring partial discharge events in GIS systems in real-time [4]. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) have emerged as highly promising materials for environmental, energy, and health applications. They exhibit quantum confinement effect, large surface-to-volume ratio, adjustable band gap, excellent conductivity, chemical stability, and minimal cytotoxicity [5]. Their distinctive optical and electronic properties have enabled its utilization in various applications such as gas sensors, biosensors, photovoltaic systems, and energy storage devices [6].

Graphene-based sensors have shown exceptional sensitivity [7,8], making them a promising option for high-performance gas sensors. The robust sp2 and π-electron conjugation in pristine GQD limits its reactivity towards toxic molecules and reduces its detection capacity [9,10]. Introducing a single metal atom into graphene makes it a highly active catalyst for many reactions with remarkable reactivity and selectivity. However, the weak interaction of metal atoms with pristine GQD allows them to agglomerate into large clusters, limiting its utility. Introducing structural defects and non-metal dopants has proven to be effective in tuning surface reactivity and physical properties, along with strong metal-GQD interaction [11–15].

Graphene divacancy (555–777) defects, consisting of three pentagons and three heptagons, are found to be more stable compared to other double vacancy defects [16]. Incorporating heteroatoms into these sites enhances stability, facilitates charge redistribution, and promotes free electron availability, improving interactions with metal atoms and adsorbates. This assertion is supported with research conducted by Tang et al. [17,18], who concluded that palladium (Pd)-supported N-doped 555–777 graphene, as well as cobalt (Co) and platinum (Pt) decorated heteroatom-doped 555–777 graphene, exhibit promising adsorption and charge transfer characteristics for various target gases.

Further, silicon (Si)-doped GQDs have sparked considerable interest in the scientific community, especially following the successful synthesis of graphene featuring 555–777 defects infused with three Si atoms [19]. Numerous research groups have explored how these defects and Si doping, further decorated with metal atoms (Fe, Ni, Co and Pd) enhances the adsorption properties of the graphene. Their studies primarily target applications involving CO oxidation [20–22] and the adsorption of harmful gases such as HCN, NH3, and SO2 [23]. Additionally, the Si atoms, which share a similar electronic configuration with carbon (C) atoms, play a crucial role in stabilizing metal atoms and affecting their interactions with the graphene framework. Interestingly, a single platinum (Pt) atom within the 3Si-graphene sheet shows greater stability when compared to a Pt atom on the 3N and 3P graphene sheets [24]. Furthermore, ruthenium (Ru) atoms are well-known for their high catalytic activity, selectivity, and both thermal and chemical stability, making them a promising alternative to expensive Pt-based catalysts. When Ru is atomically dispersed on N-doped graphene, it demonstrates exceptional catalytic performance for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic media [25]. Researchers have also synthesized a Si-GQD/Ru3+ complex that effectively detects triclosan [26]. Additionally, Ru-based transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) are being investigated for their potential as sensors in lung cancer diagnosis through the analysis of exhaled gases [27], as well as for real-time monitoring of dissolved gases in transformer oil [28]. Therefore, exploring the adsorption and sensing capabilities of Ru decorated 3Si doped defective GQD (Ru@3Si-GQD) towards SF6 decomposition gases together with background SF6 gas would be intriguing.

This investigation focuses on developing a more effective and reliable methodology for the adsorption and sensing of SF6 and its decomposition gases, including H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2, through density functional theory (DFT). Our interest lies in examining the doping of three Si atoms together with decorating Ru atom on the surface of the defective GQD (Ru@3Si-GQD). This approach presents a novel strategy for creating low-cost, high-performance modified GQDs for sensing applications. This process involved examining key factors like adsorption energy, charge transfer dynamics, frontier molecular orbitals, and density of states. We have evaluated the practicality of the Ru@3Si-GQD sensor by calculating the work function, selectivity, sensitivity, and desorption time. We have used methods such as quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM), non-covalent interactions (NCI) and electron localization function (ELF) to understand the interactions between the surface and the target gases. Our research identified a gap in analyses regarding the sensitivity of Ru@3Si-GQD towards SF6 and its decomposed gases. As far as we know, this has not been done before on the Ru@3Si-GQD substrate. The results indicate potential improvements in gas sensor technologies and the development of biocompatible materials with better gas adsorption properties.

All density functional theory (DFT) calculations were done using Gaussian 16, following standard convergence criteria [29]. We have used the ωB97XD functional for geometry optimizations. This functional effectively incorporates long-range interactions and adds corrections for van der Waals (vdW) forces. We have combined it with a composite basis set that includes an all-electron Pople basis set [6-31G(d,p)] and a pseudopotential basis set (LanL2DZ). The LanL2DZ basis set applies the Los Alamos effective core potential for the ruthenium atom, while the 6-31G(d,p) basis set is used for all other atoms. Many research groups widely use this approach for calculating electronic structures related to non-covalent interactions of carbon-based materials [10,30,31]. Additionally, vibrational frequency analysis at this theoretical level confirmed that the optimized geometries are at true minima because there are no imaginary frequencies.

To ensure the stability of the Ru atom, it is essential to calculate its binding energy with respect to the 3Si-GQD system. The binding energy is defined as follows:
(1)
E b
=
E
Ru @ 3Si − GQD
–
E Ru
+
E
3Si − GQD
Where E Ru@3Si-GQD, E Ru, and E 3Si-GQD represent the energies of Ru decorated 3Si-GQD, a Ru atom, and an 3Si-GQD, respectively.

The adsorption energy (Eads) of the target gases towards the surface of 3Si-GQD is determined through the following expression,
(2)
E ads
=
E
Ru @ 3Si − GQD + gas
–
E
Ru @ 3Si − GQD
+
E gas
Where E ads is the adsorption energy of the adsorbate on the adsorbent. E Ru@3Si-GQD+gas is the energy of adsorbate-adsorbent complex, E Ru@3Si-GQD is the energy of Ru decorated 3Si-GQD and E gas is the energy of SF6 and its decomposed gases. A negative adsorption energy denotes the favourable interaction of the studied gas molecule with Ru-decorated GQDs.

The Atomic Dipole Moment Corrected Hirshfeld (ADCH) charge analysis [32,33], performed using Multiwfn [34], reveals insights into the charge transfer between the Ru@3Si-GQD surface and the target gases. Further, to better understand the interaction dynamics between the Ru@3Si-GQD surface and the target gases, the charge density difference (CDD) is calculated using the following equation:
(3)
∆ ρ =
ρ
Ru @ 3 Si − GQD + gas
−
ρ
Ru @ 3 Si − GQD
−
ρ gas
where
ρ
Ru @ 3 Si − GQD + gas
is the charge density of the adsorbed gas molecules on the Ru @3Si-GQD surface,
ρ
Ru @ 3 Si − GQD
is the charge density of the surface without the molecules and
ρ gas
is the charge density of the target gases.

Furthermore, we have obtained the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) using frontier molecular orbital (FMO) theory. We have obtained visual representations of the 3D- isosurface of HOMO and LUMO with GaussView [35]. We have also analyzed the density of states (DOS) and generated DOS spectra using Multiwfn. This analysis helped us better understand the mechanisms of sensing and adsorption by providing information on the number of available energy states for electrons at specific energy levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of Ru@3Si-GQD and target gases
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(a) illustrates the double vacancy graphene quantum dot (DV-GQD) featuring a 555–777 rearrangement, which includes three pairs of heptagons and pentagons. In the next step, a C atom was removed from the centre, and its three nearest neighbouring C atoms were substituted with Si atoms to create a model that aligns with experimental observations [19], as shown in Fig. 1(b). The inclusion of Si atoms plays a vital role in stabilizing the vacancy in the GQD by eliminating dangling bonds. The modified GQD is further decorated with Ru atom at the vacancy site to enhance its reactivity and adsorption properties. The addition of a Ru atom positioned above the vacancy leads to the formation of a novel substrate, Ru@3Si-GQD, as illustrated in Fig. 1(c). Unlike C and Si atoms, the Ru atom has a larger atomic radius, causing it to displace slightly from the 3Si-GQD plane, while the neighbouring Si atoms experience a minor upward shift. This pull-out structure may reduce spatial constraints during the adsorption process, ultimately enhancing the overall adsorption properties. DFT calculations demonstrated that the binding energy (Eb) of Ru@3Si-GQD, as calculated using eq. (1), is −7.29 eV. This value exceeds the cohesive energy of bulk Ru, which is −6.74 eV based on experimental findings [36]. This indicates that the Ru@3Si-GQD configuration is sufficiently stable at room temperature. The negative binding energy signifies that energy is released during the formation of the bond, suggesting exothermic binding and enhanced bond stability. Additionally, the ADCH analysis revealed that Ru exhibits electron-donating characteristics when bonding with 3Si-GQD, contributing 0.233e. The significant amount of charge transfer implies the formation of a new bond between Ru and the 3Si-GQD substrate, reflecting a substantial degree of electron hybridization. Furthermore, the band gap (Eg) analysis indicates an increase from 5.47 eV for 3Si-GQD to 5.92 eV for Ru@3Si-GQD, resulting in an increase of 0.45 eV in the Eg with the addition of a single Ru atom.

Additionally, prior to gas adsorption, the geometrical structures of the target gases i.e. SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2 were optimized to determine their most stable configurations. When SF6 molecules decompose, they produce various sulfur-fluorine compounds that can easily react with moisture and oxygen present in the environment. This process results in a range of sulfur-containing gas molecules, typically involving five gas molecules composed of four distinct elements: sulfur (S), fluorine (F), oxygen (O), and hydrogen (H). The optimized structures of the target gases are illustrated in Fig. 1(d-i). The gas molecules exhibit symmetry around the S atom. In SF6, the SF bond length measures 1.59 Å, and the F-S-F bond angle is 90°. In contrast, the HF molecule has a distance of 0.92 Å between H and F atoms. Both H2S and SO2 molecules possess V-shaped structures, although they differ in bond lengths and angles. The H-S-H angle in H2S is measured at 93.02°, with an HS bond length of 1.34 Å. Similarly, the SO2 molecule features an O-S-O angle of 118.95°, attributable to the presence of covalent bonds, with an SO bond length of 1.45 Å. Furthermore, both SO2F2 and SOF2 have symmetrical arrangements of S and O atoms around a central point, resulting in a comparatively similar physical size. SO2F2 adopts a tetrahedral shape, with SO and SF bond lengths of 1.42 Å and 1.57 Å, respectively. The bond angles are measured as follows: O-S-O at 125.14°, O-S-F at 108.15°, and F-S-F at 94.87°. On the other hand, SOF2 forms a pyramidal shape, with SO and SF bond lengths of 1.43 Å and 1.61 Å, respectively. The bond angles in SOF2 are obtained as 106.93° for O-S-F and 92.49° for F-S-F. The structural properties determined in this study align with previous findings [37,38].

The optimized structures of the gas-Ru@3Si-GQD system are illustrated in Fig. 2 (a-f). During the simulation, the target gas molecules were positioned approximately 3 Å above the Ru atom initially. To evaluate the stability of the adsorbate-adsorbent systems, we have analyzed the adsorption energy (Eads) using eq. (2) for the target gases: SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2, as presented in Fig. 3 . All calculated adsorption energies are negative, indicating stability. The adsorption energy values for SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2 on Ru@3Si-GQD are −0.24 eV, −0.88 eV, −0.75 eV, −0.94 eV, −0.19 eV, and − 1.00 eV, respectively. From these values, it can be concluded that SF6 and SO2F2 are primarily adsorbed through physisorption, while H2S, HF, SO2, and SOF2 are adsorbed via chemisorption. Following the adsorption on Ru@3Si-GQD, the SF6 molecule was observed to be positioned above the Ru atom at a distance of 2.55 Å. This adsorption resulted in a change in the bond length between S and F, increasing from 1.59 Å to 1.61 Å. The H2S molecule was captured with an adsorption distance of 2.49 Å from the Ru atom, showing a slight alteration of 0.01 Å in the HS bond length. The HF molecule stabilized above the Ru atom at a distance of 2.39 Å, elongating the HF bond length from 0.92 Å to 0.93 Å. Upon the adsorption of the SO2 molecule above the Ru atom, a chemisorption process led to an increase in the SO bond length from 1.45 Å to 1.51 Å. The interaction between SO2F2 and Ru@3Si-GQD resulted in an intermolecular distance of 2.62 Å from the Ru atom, with the SF bond length changing from 1.57 Å to 1.60 Å. The SOF2 molecule exhibited strong adsorption above the Ru atom, recording the shortest adsorption distance of 2.26 Å among the target gases. The bond lengths for SO and SF in the isolated molecules were increased from 1.43 Å to 1.44 Å and from 1.61 Å to 1.62 Å, respectively. The adsorption distances correspond with the adsorption energies, following the order: SO2F2 > SF6 > H2S > HF > SO2 > SOF2. Thus, a stronger adsorption correlates with a shorter adsorption distance (Fig. 3).

The non-covalent interactions (NCI) between the adsorbed gas and the Ru@3Si-GQD surface play a crucial role in influencing the adsorption and desorption of gas molecules. The strength of these interactions determines the energy required for a target gas molecule to either bind to or release from the surface, subsequently affecting the desorption time of the sensor. Therefore, the desorption time of a gas sensor is significantly impacted by NCI interactions. Understanding and controlling these interactions between the adsorbate and the adsorbent is essential for enhancing the performance of gas sensors. The analysis of non-covalent interactions provides a methodological framework to elucidate the weak interactions present between the target gas and the Ru@3Si-GQD complex. This analytical approach involves the use of a three-dimensional isosurface and a two-dimensional scatter plot of the reduced density gradient (RDG). The NCI scatter plot depicts the RDG as a function of sign (λ2)ρ, which is derived from electron density and its first derivative [39] given in eq. 4:
(4)
RDG
r
=
1
2
3
π 2
1 / 3
∣ ∇ ρ
r
∣
ρ
r
4 ∕ 3
Where ∇ , ρ
r
, and ∣ ∇ ρ
r
∣ represent the gradient operator, electron density, and the modulus of the gradient operator applied to the electron density, respectively. The value of ρ
r
reflects the strength of the bond, while the expression sign (λ2)ρ is utilized to predict the nature of interactions. In this analysis, strong attractive interactions are indicated by sign (λ2)ρ < 0, repulsive non-bonded interactions by sign (λ2)ρ > 0, and weak van der Waals forces are approximated by sign (λ2)ρ ≈ 0. To effectively differentiate among these categories of non-covalent interactions (NCIs), a colour-coding scheme is employed: blue denotes strong electrostatic interactions, red indicates steric repulsion, and green represents van der Waals interactions.

The 2D RDG scatter plots illustrating the interaction between the target gases (SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2) and the Ru@3Si-GQD surface are presented in Fig. 4 (a-f). Fig. 4a shows the weak electrostatic and van der Waals (vdW) interactions between SF6 and the Ru@3Si-GQD surface, indicated by the presence of green and blue spikes at sign (λ2)ρ < 0. Additionally, H2S gas interacts with the Ru@3Si-GQD surface through vdW and electrostatic interactions, as evidenced by a sharp green spike near the −0.01 a.u. region and blue scatter points in sign (λ2)ρ < 0, as depicted in Fig. 4b. The NCI plots (Fig. 4c and e) further confirm that the adsorption of HF and SO2F2 on the Ru@3Si-GQD surface is characterized by both weak electrostatic and vdW interactions, supported by the density of blue and green spikes observed in the 2D RDG maps. Moreover, the 2D RDG scatter plot in Fig. 4d and f reveals blue peaks at sign (λ2)ρ < 0, indicating that strong electrostatic contributions towards the Ru@3Si-GQD surface predominantly govern the adsorption of SO2 and SOF2. Thus, NCI analysis complements the findings from the adsorption energy analysis. It is important to note that the interaction between Ru@3Si-GQD substrate and H2S, HF, SO2, and SOF2 gases is classified as chemisorption. In contrast, SF6 and SO2F2 gas molecules are primarily adsorbed via physisorption. This distinction deepens our understanding of the underlying adsorption mechanisms and may guide future research directions in this field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and electron localization function (ELF) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To gain deeper insights into the interaction between target gases (SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2) gases and the Ru@3Si-GQD surface, we have employed a topology analysis based on Bader's quantum theory of atoms, utilizing Multiwfn. The QTAIM analysis is a well-established methodology that thoroughly explains electronic density distribution. This analytical approach is a valuable tool for elucidating adsorption processes and predicting the behaviours of various molecules on different surfaces. Within the QTAIM framework, evaluating electron density at bond critical points (BCPs) between interacting atoms is crucial for determining the strength of bonding interactions [40,41]. The QTAIM analysis employs various topological factors to characterize interactions, including the electron density ρ(r), the Laplacian of electron density ∇2ρ(r), Lagrangian kinetic energy G(r), potential energy density V(r), and energy density H(r) at the bond critical point. A bond critical point (BCP) characterized by ∇2ρ(r) > 0 and H(r) > 0 indicates a non-covalent interaction. In contrast, conditions where ∇2ρ(r) > 0 and H(r) < 0 suggest a partial covalent interaction. Finally, when ∇2ρ(r) < 0 and H(r) < 0, this reflects a covalent type of interaction [42]. As shown in Table 1 , the QTAIM analysis reveals that the interaction of target gases (SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2) with the Ru@3Si-GQD surface exhibits a partial covalent nature. This is supported by the findings that ∇2ρ(r) > 0 and H(r) < 0 for each bond critical point (BCP). Furthermore, the ratio of Lagrangian kinetic energy G(r) to potential energy density V(r) falls within the range of 0.5 to 1, reinforcing this conclusion. Values less than 0.5, between 0.5 and 1, and greater than 1 correspond to covalent interactions, partial covalent interactions, and non-covalent interactions, respectively [43].

Additionally, we have conducted an analysis employing the electron localization function (ELF) to investigate the adsorption properties. ELF is a valuable tool for assessing electron localization within atomic and molecular systems, which enhances our understanding of the nature of chemical bonding. The ELF colour-filled maps that illustrate the interactions between the adsorbent and adsorbate are presented in Fig. S1(a-f). An ELF contour value of 1, depicted in red, indicates complete electron localization, while a value of 0, shown in blue, represents total electron delocalization. In conjunction with the analysis of adsorption energy, we observe that a higher degree of electron localization between the target gas and the Ru@3Si-GQD surface corresponds to higher adsorption energy. The minimal electron localization between SF6/SO2F2 gases and the Ru@3Si-GQD surface suggests that the interaction is primarily influenced by weak forces. Conversely, the significant degree of electron localization between the Ru atom and the S atom in SO2 and SOF2 gases indicates the formation of a partial covalent bond between the Ru atom and the S atom of the gas. Our findings from the ELF analysis are consistent with those derived from QTAIM and NCI analyses.

Additionally, we have conducted an analysis employing the electron localization function (ELF) to investigate the adsorption properties. ELF is a valuable tool for assessing electron localization within atomic and molecular systems, which enhances our understanding of the nature of chemical bonding. The ELF colour-filled maps that illustrate the interactions between the adsorbent and adsorbate are presented in Fig. S1(a-f). An ELF contour value of 1, depicted in red, indicates complete electron localization, while a value of 0, shown in blue, represents total electron delocalization. In conjunction with the analysis of adsorption energy, we observe that a higher degree of electron localization between the target gas and the Ru@3Si-GQD surface corresponds to higher adsorption energy. The minimal electron localization between SF6/SO2F2 gases and the Ru@3Si-GQD surface suggests that the interaction is primarily influenced by weak forces. Conversely, the significant degree of electron localization between the Ru atom and the S atom in SO2 and SOF2 gases indicates the formation of a partial covalent bond between the Ru atom and the S atom of the gas. Our findings from the ELF analysis are consistent with those derived from QTAIM and NCI analyses.

To quantitatively and visually analyze the transfer of electronic charge between target gases (SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2) and the Ru@3Si-GQD surface, we have performed an ADCH charge analysis. Using Eq. (3), we have generated and plotted the CDD diagrams presented in Fig. 5 (a-f). A cyan isosurface indicates charge depletion, while a yellow isosurface signifies charge accumulation regions. In Fig. 5a, the cyan isosurface appears above and below the Ru atom, extending towards the F atoms of SF6, whereas the yellow isosurface is located over Si atoms, indicating that 0.12e of charge has been transferred from the SF6 gas to the Ru@3Si-GQD surface. Fig. 5b reveals that the 3Si-GQD and Ru predominantly display the yellow isosurface, in contrast to the H2S gas, which mainly shows the cyan isosurface. The presence of a yellow isosurface on the Ru and 3Si-GQD suggests that the Ru@3Si-GQD surface has gained 0.18e of charge from the H2S gas. Fig. 5c illustrates a similar arrangement, where the 3Si-GQD isosurface is depicted in yellow, extending beneath the Ru atom. The cyan isosurface is positioned above the Ru atom and adjacent H and F atoms. This suggests that HF gas donated a charge of 0.09e to both the Ru atom and the 3Si-GQD surface. Additionally, in Fig. 5d, the cyan isosurface is visible both above and below the Ru atom, extending towards the 3Si-GQD surface, indicating the charge shared by the Ru@3Si-GQD surface and the SO2 gas. The yellow isosurfaces over SO2 signify that it has gained a charge of 0.19e from the Ru@3Si-GQD surface. The significant charge transfer between SO2 and Ru@3Si-GQD, along with a short adsorption distance and high adsorption energy, indicates a strong interaction between the two. This interaction can alter the electronic structure of the system, affecting its properties and performance. In Fig. 5e, the yellow isosurface appears above and below the Ru atom, while the cyan isosurface is present over the SO2F2 gas, indicating that SO2F2 transferred 0.09e of charge to the Ru@3Si-GQD surface. Finally, in Fig. 5f, the cyan isosurface can be seen on the 3Si-GQD surface and atop the Ru atom, while the yellow isosurface is associated with the SOF2 gas. This suggests that SOF2 acts as an electron acceptor, receiving a charge of 0.05e.

The inclusion of Ru atoms modifies the distribution of HOMO/LUMO on the 3Si-GQD surface. This indicates that Ru atoms enhance the charge distribution both around the individual Ru atom sites and on the intrinsic 3Si-GQD surface. Given the strong charge transfer capabilities of the Ru atom adsorption sites, they may serve as reactive sites for target gas adsorption. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , prior to gas adsorption, the distribution of HOMO and LUMO is predominantly localized on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD. Following the adsorption of target gases on this surface, a significant change occurs in the distribution of HOMO and LUMO. The shift in HOMO-LUMO levels resulting from the interaction signifies a charge transfer process associated with the interaction of the Ru@3Si-GQD surface with target gases such as SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2. As illustrated in Fig. 6, we have estimated the HOMO and LUMO energies alongside the variation in the band gap. Following the interaction with SF6, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of Ru@3Si-GQD changed from −6.712 eV and − 0.795 eV to −6.656 eV and − 0.789 eV, respectively, resulting in a shift of 0.05 eV in the band gap. Upon the adsorption of H2S onto Ru@3Si-GQD, the energies of the HOMO and LUMO shift to −6.662 eV and − 0.807 eV, respectively, resulting in a bandgap change of 0.062 eV. Similar modifications have been observed for the HF/Ru@3Si-GQD, with HOMO and LUMO energy levels now at −6.749 eV and − 0.927 eV, respectively. Additionally, the Ru@3Si-GQD surface exhibits a significant alteration in HOMO and LUMO energy levels to −7.053 eV and − 1.287 eV following the adsorption of SO2, leading to a bandgap change of 0.151 eV. However, there are no abrupt changes in the band gap of Ru@3Si-GQD following the adsorption of SO2F2. Additionally, the SOF2/Ru@3Si-GQD complex shows the most significant change in Eg (0.16 eV), suggesting that chemisorption occurs between them. The localization of the HOMO and LUMO electron densities for SOF2 adsorbed on Ru@3Si-GQD indicates optimal adsorption energies between the two.

The analysis of the density of states (DOS) was performed to deepen our understanding of the adsorption phenomena of the target gases on the surface of the Ru@3Si-GQD adsorbent. This DOS analysis elucidates the contributions of atomic or molecular orbital fragments that enable interactions at a quantum level within a designated energy range, as illustrated in the corresponding DOS plots. In Figs. 7 (a-f), the magenta curve represents the DOS associated with the target gas and the Ru@3Si-GQD complex after the adsorption. The colour filled blue curve corresponds to the DOS of the Ru@3Si-GQD adsorbent prior to the adsorption process. The data presented in Figs. 7(a-f) clearly indicate that after the adsorption of target gases such as SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2, the DOS of the adsorption system experiences a slight shift to the left. The DOS curves remain relatively unchanged near the Fermi level following the adsorption of SF6 and SO2F2 onto the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD. A minimal shift in the DOS near the Fermi level is observed following the adsorption of H2S and HF gases. In contrast, the most significant changes in the DOS near the Fermi level are noted for SO2 and SOF2 gases, indicating a strong interaction between the electronic states of the Ru@3Si-GQD surface and these gases. This finding is consistent with the results from adsorption energy and FMO analysis. Furthermore, it suggests that the alterations in the electronic structure of the Ru@3Si-GQD system contribute to the generation of electrical signals.

The Ru@3Si-GQD shows a strong ability to adsorb specific gases, according to the analysis results. To better understand its sensitivity and selectivity towards target gases (SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2), we have calculated the bandgap, work function, and desorption time for six different adsorption systems.

The work function is the energy needed to move an electron from the fermi level to a vacuum, which removes it from the material's surface. We have calculated the work function (Φ) using Eq. 5:
(5)
Φ =
E v
−
E F
Where
E v
,
E F
represent the energy corresponding to the vacuum and Fermi levels, respectively. When
E v
=0, Φ = −
E F
[44,45]. Table 2 depicts the change in work function (|∆Φ|) of Ru@3Si-GQD following the adsorption of target gases. This variation in work function reflects changes in the material's conductivity, which can disrupt the gate voltage of the Kelvin oscillator [44]. It is noteworthy that the system's sensitivity to gas adsorption leads to a more significant variation in work function before and after adsorption [46].

This suggests that the work function can be adjusted through selective gas adsorption. According to Table 2, the work function of Ru@3Si-GQD is 3.754 eV. The values for the work function across various gas adsorption systems are 3.723 eV, 3.735 eV, 3.838 eV, 4.17 eV, 3.757 eV, and 4.09 eV for the adsorption of SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2, respectively. When SOF2 interacts with the adsorbent, a change of 0.336 eV in the work function is observed. Additionally, for SO2, the Ru@3Si-GQD surface displays the highest |∆Φ| of 0.416 eV, indicating greater selectivity towards the SO2 and SOF2 gases for sensing purposes. Therefore, by analyzing the work function of Ru@3Si-GQD before and after gas adsorption, it can be concluded that Ru@3Si-GQD holds great potential as a field effect transistor (FET) sensor for the selective detection of SO2 and SOF2, among other gases decomposed from SF6.

To evaluate the suitability of Ru@3Si-GQD as a sensor for target gases (SF6, H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2), sensitivity (S) has been assessed by examining changes in the band gap. The variations in the band gap (∆Eg) were determined by calculating the difference between the band gap after the adsorption of the target gas (Eg (Ru@3Si-GQD+gas)) and the band gap before adsorption (Eg(Ru@3Si-GQD)), using eq. 6:
(6)
∆E g
=
E
g
Ru @ 3Si − GQD + gas
–
E
g
Ru @ 3Si − GQD

The band gap (∆Eg) change before and after gas adsorption is commonly documented as an indicator for understanding resistance fluctuations in the adsorption system [47]. Sensitivity (S) has been calculated using Eq. 7 [48]:
(7)
S =
e
∆
E g
2 KT
− 1
Where, K represents the Boltzmann constant (8.62 × 10−5 eV/k), and T denotes the system's working temperature. The sensitivity (S) of the target gases on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD, derived from eq. (7), is presented in Table 2. The Ru@3Si-GQD sensor exhibits a significant electrical response to SO2 and SOF2 gases. These results demonstrate that Ru@3Si-GQD has excellent applicability as a sensor for SO2 and SOF2 gases, with sensitivities of approximately 18.03 and 21.54, respectively. Interestingly, the sensitivities for the other four gases (SF6, H2S, HF and SO2F2) are quite minimal. The results are in accordance with the FMO analysis, demonstrating that Ru@3Si-GQD exhibits exceptional selectivity and sensitivity towards SO2 and SOF2 gases.

The gas desorption process is important for reusing gas-sensitive materials and measuring sensor performance. We have calculated the desorption time (τ) using the Van't Hoff-Arrhenius equation and transition state theory [49], as shown in eq. 8:
(8)
τ =
A
− 1
exp
−
E a
KT
where, A is the apparent frequency factor (1012 s−1). Desorption, which is the reverse of adsorption, is defined by the desorption energy barrier (Ea), typically aligned with the adsorption energy (Eads) [50]. According to Eq. 8, the desorption time is related to both the sensor's operating temperature and the adsorption energy of the target gases on the surface of the adsorbent. For effective reuse of gas-sensitive materials, the adsorption energies of the adsorbed gas molecules should fall within the range of −0.30 eV to −0.80 eV, which is considered suitable for adsorption energy [42,51].

We have determined the temperature-dependent desorption times of Ru@3Si-GQD for various target gas molecules at 298 K, 398 K, and 498 K, as shown in Table 2. At room temperature (298 K), the gas molecules SO2F2, SF6, and HF exhibit rapid desorption times of 1.56 ns, 11.7 ns, and 4.06 s, respectively, from the Ru@3Si-GQD surface. Such short desorption times render the adsorbent suitable for use as a reusable sensor material. In contrast, the gases H2S, SO2, and SOF2 require significantly longer desorption times of 0.826 ks, 7.9 ks, and 87.7 ks, respectively, indicating that the adsorbent effectively functions as a scavenger for these targeted gas molecules due to the prolonged desorption times. As indicated in Table 2, the SO2F2/Ru@3Si-GQD system demonstrates the shortest desorption time of 1.56 ns, highlighting its superior desorption properties. This rapid desorption time for SO2F2 correlates with the lowest adsorption energy observed among all configurations analyzed. As temperature increases, the kinetic energy and diffusion rate of gas molecules also rise, leading to a significant decrease in desorption time [45]. At 398 K, the gases H2S, SO2, and SOF2 easily desorb from the Ru@3Si-GQD substrate in 0.15 s, 0.8 s, and 4.85 s, respectively. Consequently, Ru@3Si-GQD shows great potential as a reusable gas sensor for detecting H2S, SO2, and SOF2 at elevated temperatures.

The adsorption and sensing mechanisms of five SF6 decomposition gases, namely H2S, HF, SO2, SO2F2, and SOF2, as well as the background gas SF6, on the Ru@3Si-GQD surface, have been studied using DFT. The main findings have been summarized as follows:
(i)
The adsorption energies of all six target gases on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD are negative, indicating stability. Furthermore, it can be concluded that SF6 and SO2F2 are primarily adsorbed through physisorption, while H2S, HF, SO2, and SOF2 are adsorbed via chemisorption.
(ii)
The charge transfer analysis reveals that SF6, H2S, HF, and SO2F2 function as electron donor gases, whereas SO2 and SOF2 are identified as electron acceptor gases towards the Ru@3Si-GQD surface. This understanding could enhance our insights into the interactions between the target gases and the surface, paving the way for further research and applications.
(iii)
The electronic redistribution leads to variations in the bandgap and work function of the Ru@3Si-GQD surface following gas adsorption. Considering these changes in bandgap and work function, Ru@3Si-GQD demonstrates exceptional selectivity and sensitivity as an efficient gas sensor for detecting SO2 and SOF2 at 298 K.
(iv)
At 298 K, Ru@3Si-GQD demonstrates significant potential as a gas scavenger for effectively removing decomposed SF6 gases, including H2S, SO2, and SOF2, due to its prolonged desorption times. Additionally, it serves as a reusable sensor material for SF6, HF, and SO2F2 gases. However, it is a reusable gas sensor for detecting H2S, SO2, and SOF2 at higher temperatures.
(v)
We have proposed Ru@3Si-GQD as a novel material for effective biocompatible gas sensors to detect SF6 decomposition products, ensuring the safe operation of SF6-insulated devices such as GIS.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Electron localization function (ELF) analysis for the adsorption of the target gases i.e. (a) SF6, (b) H2S, (c) HF, (d) SO2, (e) SO2F2 and (f) SOF2 on the surface of Ru@3Si-GQD respectively.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2025.111975.

Saurav Kumar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Neha Agnihotri: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Establishing the accuracy of broadly used density Functionals in describing bulk properties of transition metals
